text:
Sure, here are some interesting spots in Copenhagen:

Amarienborg: This is the home of the Danish royal family. You can witness the change of guard at 12:00. The admission price is $1.25 and you can reach there within 10 minutes from the Copenhagen airport. It opens all week.

Frederiks Kirke: This is a famous modern architecture. The admission price is $20 and it opens from Tuesday to Friday.

The Little Mermaid Statue: This is the character from Grimm's Fairy Tales. The admission is free and it opens all week.

Rundetaarn: This is an Astronomy tower from the 17th century. The admission price is DKK 40 and it opens all week.

Christiansborg Palace: This is one of the most significant landmarks in Copenhagen, both historically and politically. The admission is free and it opens from Tuesday to Sunday.

National Museum of Denmark: This is the country’s largest cultural history museum, offering an extensive collection of artifacts and exhibits that showcase Denmark’s rich history. The admission price is $10 and it opens from Tuesday to Sunday.

The type of power sockets used in Denmark are Type A and Type K. Make sure to bring an adapter if your devices use different types of plugs.
The type of power sockets used in Denmark are Type A and Type K. Make sure to bring an adapter if your devices use different types of plugs.
The official currency in Denmark is the Danish Krone (DKK). Most places do not accept euros, so be prepared to exchange your money or use a credit card.
The official currency in Denmark is the Danish Krone (DKK). Most places do not accept euros, so be prepared to exchange your money or use a credit card.
There are in total 6 metros in Copenhagen. You can reach anywhere by metro. This will be especially useful for getting to places like the Amarienborg or the National Museum of Denmark.

The weather in Copenhagen in December is below -15 degrees Celsius. Dress warmly if you're planning to visit during this time.
The weather in Copenhagen in December is below -15 degrees Celsius. Dress warmly if you're planning to visit during this time.
The official language is Danish, but English is widely spoken in Copenhagen. You should have no problem communicating at any of the spots.
The official language is Danish, but English is widely spoken in Copenhagen. You should have no problem communicating at any of the spots.
Remember, the “hello” in Danish is “hej”, “thank you” in Danish is “farvel”, and “goodbye” in Danish is “tak”.
